title: Comparison of Protein Separations in Capillary Zone Electrophoresis and Capillary Isoelectric Focusing Interfacing with Electrospray Mass Spectrometry
abstract:
JOURNAL OF MASS SPECTROMETRY, VOL. 31, 1284-1290 (1996) 
Comparison of Protein Separations in Capillary 
Zone Electrophoresis and Capillary Isoelectric 
Focusing Interfacing with Electrospray Mass 
Spectrometry 
Qing Tang, A. Kame1 Harrata and Cheng S. Lee* 
Department of Chemistry and Ames Laboratory USDOE, Iowa State University, Ames, IA 50011, USA 
On-line capillary zone electrophoresis/electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (CZE-ESMS) and capillary iso- 
electric focusing/electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (CIEF/ESMS) were employed for protein analysis. The 
separation mechanisms and the detection limits of CZE/ESMS and CIEF/ESMS were compared by using model 
proteins including cytochrome c (horse heart), ribonuclease A (bovine pancreas), myoglobin (horse heart), carbonic 
anhydrase I (human erythrocytes) and P-lactoglobulin A (bovine milk). The effect of a moving ionic boundary 
inside the electrophoresis capillary on the separation resolution of model proteins by CZE/ESMS and CIEF/ESMS 
is described. In CZE/ESMS, the formation of a moving ionic boundary due to the replacement of background 
electrolyte counterions with sheath liquid counterions can be eliminated by using a common counterion in both the 
background electrolyte and the sheath liquid. Additionally, the moving ionic boundary in CIEF/ESMS is minimized 
by combining cathodic mobilization with a gravity-induced hydrodynamic flow. The concentration detection Limits 
of model proteins in a full-scan CIEF/ESMS analysis are in the region of 
M, N 20-50 times lower than that 
achievable using CZE/ESMS. Sample preconcentration taking place during the focusing step in CIEF is 
responsible for the improved detection limits. 
KEYWORDS: capillary zone electrophoresis; capillary isoelectric focusing; electrospray ionization mass spec- 
trometry 
INTRODUCTION 
The use of a capillary as the electrophoretic migration 
channel in high-performance capillary electrophoresis 
(HPCE) exhibits the advantages of small sample 
requirement, high separation eniciency and resolution 
and rapid sample analysis. Capillary zone electro- 
phoresis (CZE) and capillary isoelectric focusing (CIEF) 
arc the most frequently used modes of HPCE for 
protein separations. CZE resolves protein analytes 
mainly on the basis of their charge and to a lesser 
degree on size. In CIEF separations, the capillary con- 
tains not only amphoiytes for the formation of a pH 
gradient but also the protein sample to be analyzed. 
The proteins are focused into discrete and narrow zones 
with local pHs corresponding to their isoelectric points 
The combination of HPCE with electrospray mass 
spectrometry (ESMS) is very attractive for obtaining 
higher selectivity and for structural analysis of protein 
molecules using MS/MS. Different CZE/ESMS inter- 
faces have been developed, including the use of metal- 
lized capillary terminus, sheath (coaxial) interface, liquid 
junction and electrical contact through use of a gold 
The use of any CZE/MS interface is to estab- 
lish the electrical connection at the CZE capillary ter- 
(PIS). 
*To whom all correspondence should be addressed. 
minus, which serves to define the electric field along the 
CZE capillary. The electrical connection at the capillary 
terminus is also used to apply the 3-6 kV voltage at the 
electrospray needle. The comparison between sheath 
interface and liquid junction has been made by Plea- 
sance et aL7 and the coaxial sheath flow appeared to 
have several advantages. 
On-line coupling of CIEF with ESMS has been 
demonstrated by Tang et aL8 Mixtures of model pro- 
teins were focused and cathodically mobilized in a 
polyacrylamide-coated capillary. At the end of CIEF 
capillary, the mobilized protein zones were analyzed by 
ESMS using a coaxial sheath flow configuration. Addi- 
tionally, the focusing effect of CIEF permitted the 
analysis of very dilute protein samples. A typical con- 
centration factor of 50-100 times was achieved.' 
In this study, the separation mechanisms and the 
detection limits of CZE/ESMS and CIEF/ESMS were 
compared by using model proteins including cyto- 
chrome c (horse heart), ribonuclease A (bovine 
pancreas), myoglobin (horse heart), carbonic anhydrase 
I (human erythrocytes) and j-lactoglobulin A (bovine 
milk). Formation of moving ionic boundary inside the 
CIEF capillary as observed in CZEjESMS' is the result 
of the migration of liquid sheath counterions into the 
polyacrylamide coated capillary. The moving ionic 
boundary contributes to a loss in separation resolution 
and an inversion in migration order during the CIEF/ 
ESMS experiments. Thus, the combination of cathodic 
and gravity migration of focused proteins in the CIEF 
CCC 1076-5174/96/111284-07 
0 
1996 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 
Received 30 M a y  1996 
Accepted 25 July 1996 
 COMPARISON OF PROTEIN SEPARATIONS BY CZE/ESME AND CIEF/ESMS 
1285 
capillary provides the minimization of ionic boundary 
and greatly enhances the resolving power of CIEF/ 
ESMS. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
Capillary zone electrophoresis and capillary isoelectric 
focusing with UV detection 
The capillary electrophoresis apparatus for performing 
CZE and CIEF was constructed in-house using a CZE 
lOOOR high-voltage (HV) power supply (Spellman High- 
Voltage Electronics, Plainview, NY, USA). Fused-silica 
capillaries with 50 pm i.d. and 192 pm 0.d. (Polymicro 
Technologies, Phoenix, AZ, USA) were coated inter- 
nally with linear polyacrylamide for the elimination of 
electroosmotic flow." 
CZE separation in a 40 cm long capillary was per- 
formed by applying a constant voltage of 20 kV. The 
pH of electrophoresis buffer containing 20 mM E- 
aminocaproic acid was adjusted to a value of 4.4 with 
concentrated acetic acid. The mixture of model proteins 
was prepared in the electrophoresis buffer with a final 
concentration of 
M for each protein analyte. The 
separation distance between the injection point and the 
U V  detector was 25 cm. The protein analytes were 
monitored by a UV detector from Linear Instruments 
(Reno, NV, USA) at 214 nm. 
For protein separations in CIEF format, a 30 cm 
long capillary was filled with a solution containing pro- 
teins 
and 
carrier 
ampholyte, 
Pharmalyte 
3-10 
(Pharmacia, Uppsala, Sweden). Focusing was performed 
at a 15 kV constant voltage for 15 min using 20 mM 
phosphoric acid and 20 mM sodium hydroxide as the 
anolyte and the catholyte, respectively. Cathodic mobi- 
lization was initiated by replacing the sodium hydroxide 
catholyte with a solution containing methanol-water- 
acetic acid (50: 49: 1, v/v/v) at pH 2.6. A constant 
voltage of 15 kV was applied during the mobilization. 
In comparison with CZE/UV measurements, the 
protein zones in CIEF were monitored by a UV detec- 
tor (Linear) at 280 nm, thereby reducing background 
absorbance arising from the carrier ampholytes. Owing 
to aromatic residues of tryptophan and tyrosine amino 
acids, only model proteins exhibited strong U V  absorb- 
ance at 280 nm. 
Model proteins including cytochrome c (horse heart), 
ribonuclease A (bovine pancreas), myoglobin (horse 
heart), carbonic anhydrase I (human erythrocytes) and 
fl-lactoglobulin A (bovine milk) were purchased from 
Sigma (St Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals, including 
acetic acid, e-aminocaproic acid, methanol, phosphoric 
acid and sodium hydroxide, were purchased from 
Fisher (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). All solutions were filtered 
through a 1 pm filter (Whatman, Maidstone, UK). 
Mass spectrometer and electrospray interface 
The mass spectrometer was a Finnigan MAT (San Jose, 
CA, USA) TSQ 700 triple-quadrupole equipped with an 
electrospray ionization source. The Finnigan MAT elec- 
trospray adapter kit, containing both gas and liquid 
sheath tubes, was used to couple CZE and CIEF with 
ESMS without any modification. The first quadrupole 
was used for the mass scanning of protein ions, while 
the second and third quadrupoles were operated in the 
r.f.-only mode. The electron multiplier was set at 1.4 kV 
with the conversion dynode at -15 kV. Tuning and 
calibration of the mass spectrometer were achieved 
using an acetic acid solution [methanol-water-acetic 
acid (50:49: 1, v/v/v)] containing myoglobin and a 
small peptide of methionine-arginine-phenylamalnine- 
alanine (MRFA). For both the CZE/ESMS and CIEF/ 
ESMS experiments, the first quadrupole was scanned 
from m/z 700 to 2000 with a scan rate of 2.5 s per scan. 
Capillary zone electrophoresis/electrospray mass 
spectrometry 
For the coupling of CZE with ESMS, a 30 cm long cap- 
illary was mounted within the electrospray probe. The 
capillary tip was fixed about 0.5 mm outside the electro- 
spray needle. The inlet reservoir containing a solution 
of 20 mM E-aminocaproic acid buffer at pH 4.4 was 
kept at the same height as the electrospray probe. The 
sheath liquid consisted of methanol-water-acetic acid 
(50:49: 1, v/v/v) and was delivered at a flow rate of 5 p1 
min-' by using a Harvard Apparatus (South Natick, 
MA, USA). Model 22 syringe pump. Two HV power 
supplies (Spellman) were used for delivering the electric 
potentials of 20 and 5 kV at the inlet electrode and at 
the electrospray needle, respectively. Thus, a constant 
electric field of 500 V cm-' was maintained during the 
CZE separation. Because most HV power supplies are 
not designed to operate as current sinks, a resistor 
ladder in parallel with the HV electrode connecting 
with the electrospray needle was incorporated. 
Capillary isoelectric focusing/electrospray mass 
spectrometry 
For the combination of CIEF with ESMS, a 20 cm long 
CIEF capillary was mounted within the electrospray 
probe. The outlet reservoir containing 20 mM sodium 
hydroxide as the catholyte was located inside the elec- 
trospray housing. The inlet reservoir containing 20 mM 
phosphoric acid as anolyte was kept at the same height 
as the outlet reservoir. A constant electric field of 500 V 
cm- ' was applied during the focusing step. 
Once the focusing was completed, the electric poten- 
tial was turned off and the outlet reservoir was 
removed. The capillary tip was fixed about 0.5 mm 
outside the electrospray needle. The sheath liquid com- 
position and flow rate were the same as those employed 
in the CZE/ESMS measurements. No sheath gas was 
employed for both the CZE/ESMS and CIEF/ESMS 
measurements. During the cathodic mobilization step, 
two HV power supplies (Spellman) were used for deliv- 
ering the electric potentials of 15 and 5 kV at the inlet 
electrode and at the electrospray needle, respectively. 
To combine gravity with cathodic mobilization, the 
inlet reservoir was raised 4 cm above the electrospray 
needle level.

Q. TANG, A. KAMEL HARRATA AND C. S. LEE
Capillary electrophoresis with UV detection
CZE was performed in a linear polyacrylamide-coated
capillary filled with electrophoresis buffer containing 20
mM E-aminocaproic acid at pH 4.4. Coating of the cap-
illary with linear polyacrylamide was used to suppress
electroosmosis and protein adsorption. All model pro-
teins including cytochrome c, ribonuclease A, myoglo-
bin, carbonic anhydrase I and /?-lactoglobulin A
exhibited net positive charges at pH 4.4 and migrated
towards the cathodic end, near the UV detector. The
protein mixture was electrokinetically injected at the
anodic end of the capillary by using 20 kV for 3 s. A
constant electric field equal to 500 V cm-I was then
applied for electrophoretic separation. The elution
order, as shown in (Fig. l), was cytochrome c, ribonu-
clease A, myoglobin, /?-lactoglobulin A and carbonic
anhydrase I. The apparent peak tailing of 8-
lactoglobulin A was possibly contributed to by protein
adsorption due to the hydrophobic nature of protein-
wall interaction. CZE resolved protein analytes mainly
on the basis of their charge and to a lesser degree on
In a focusing experiment, the polyacrylamide-coated
capillary contained not only 0.5% Pharmalyte 3-10 but
also model proteins. The final protein concentration in
M for each protein analyte before
the focusing. The Pharmalyte buffers contained small
amphoteric molecules of different PIS. When a constant
electric potential of 15 kV was applied, the negatively
charged acidic ampholytes migrated towards the anode
and decreased the pH at the anodic section, while the
positively charged basic ampholytes migrated towards
the cathode and increased the pH at the cathodic
section. These pH changes continued until each ionized
species had come to its PI, where it then concentrated.
Because each ampholyte had its own buffering capacity,
a virtually continuous pH gradient was formed in the
capillary. Because the proteins are amphoteric macro-
Figure 1. CZE/UV separation of model proteins. Capillary, 40 cm
total length, 50 pm i.d. and 192 krn o.d., length to detector 25 crn;
voltage, 20 kV for injection and separation; buffer, 20 mM E-
aminocaproic acid titrated with acetic acid at pH 4.4; UV detection
at 214nm. Peaks: (1) cytochrome c; (2) ribonuclease A; (3)
myoglobin; (4) &lactoglobulin A; (5) carbonic anhydrase 1.
molecules, protein analytes also focused at their PI
values in narrow zones in the same way as the individ-
ual carrier ampholytes. To prevent the carrier amp-
holytes from migrating into the inlet and outlet
reservoirs by diffusion, 20 mM phosphoric acid and 20
mM sodium hydroxide solutions were used as the
anolyte and the catholyte, respectively.
The focused protein zones were mobilized by repla-
cing the sodium hydroxide catholyte with a solution
v/v/v] at pH 2.6. The catholyte used in cathodic mobi-
lization was the same as the sheath liquid later
employed in the electrospray interface. As shown in
(Fig. 2), the elution order was myoglobin (PI 7.2 and
6.8), carbonic anhydrase I (PI 6.6) and /?-lactoglobulin A
(PI 5.1). The zone-sharpening effect, characteristic of iso-
electric focusing, made CIEF a high-resolution tool for
the analysis of hemoglobin variants with a PI difference
as small as 0.05 pH units." In comparison with CZE,
myoglobin variants with a PI difference of 0.4 pH units
were baseline separated and resolved in CIEF. For
basic proteins of cytochrome c (PI 9.6) and ribonuclease
A (PI 9.4), both protein analytes passed the UV detec-
tion window during the focusing step. Hence there were
no cytochrome c and ribonuclease A peaks in the elec-
tropherogram (see Fig. 2) monitored during cathodic
Capillary zone electrophoresis/electrospray mass
In order to maintain electroneutrality during electro-
phoretic separation, the electric charge transported by
the ions exiting one end of the separation capillary must
be substituted by a charge carried by ions of the same
sign entering the opposite end of the capillary. For the
capillaries with limited or zero electroosmotic flow
(such as the polyacrylamide-coated tubings used in this
study), the sheath liquid counterions used in the electro-
spray interface migrate into the separation capillary,
replacing the original counterions of the background
electrolyte. Thus, formation of moving ionic boundary
Figure 2. ClEFjUV separation of model proteins in the presence
of 0.5% Pharmalyte 3-1 0. Capillary, 30 cm total length, 50 p-m i.d.
and 192 prn 0.d.. length to detector 24 cm; voltage, 15 kV for
focusing and mobilization; UV detection at 280 nrn. Peaks: (1)
myoglobin (pl 7.2); (2) myoglobin (pl 6.8); (3) carbonic anhy-
drase I (pl 6.6); (4) B-lactoglobulin A (pl 5.1).
COMPARISON OF PROTEIN SEPARATIONS BY CZE/ESME AND CIEFPSMS
inside the CZE capillary was observed and theoretically
investigated by Foret et aL9 The moving ionic bound-
ary led to delays or inversions in migration order and
resulted in loss of protein resolution in CZE/ESMS.
As demonstrated by Foret et aL9 the minimization of
moving ionic boundary could be achieved by employing
a sheath liquid counterion with a pK, and electro-
phoretic mobility similar to that in the background
electrolyte. In this study, the electrophoresis buffer of 20
mM &-aminocaproic acid was titrated with acetic acid
for a final solution pH of 4.4. Hence the same acetate
ions were present in the electrophoresis buffer and in
the sheath liquid of electrospray interface. The recon-
structed ion electropherogram of protein mixture is
shown in (Fig. 3) with the concentration of
each protein analyte. In comparison with CZE-UV
measurement (see Fig. l), the longer migration distance
of 30 cm total capillary length in CZE/ESMS accounted
for the increase in the migration time. Ribonuclease A
appeared as a shoulder and overlapped with myoglobin
in the reconstructed ion electropherogram. The mixing
between the capillary eluent and the sheath liquid at the
capillary tip contributed to the decrease in the separa-
tion resolution of model proteins in CZE/ESMS. In the
absence of sheath gas, the use of a sheath flow rate of 5
pl min-' might contribute to a possible source of peak
broadening at the electrospray tip. Furthermore, the
scan rate of MS (2.5 s per scan) might be insufficient to
reflect truly the high separation efficiency of CZE.
All protein peaks were directly identified on the basis
of the mass spectra of protein analytes taken from the
average scans under the peaks. An example of the mass
spectra obtained from the average scans under the
peaks of Fig. 3 is shown in (Fig. 4) for myoglobin. Mass
detection of proteins in CZEiESMS was typically in the
high femtomole range.4*'z When the concentration of
the protein sample was considered, the detection level in
M was reported and further con-
firmed in our study (see Fig. 3). An obvious approach to
improving CZE/ESMS detection limits was to perform
sample stacking during the injection step by dissolving
the sample in water or low-concentration buffer.13 A
Figure 3. CZE/ESMS reconstructed ion electropherogram of lo-' M each of (1) cytochrome c, (2) ribonuclease A, (3) rnyoglobin, (4)
P-lactoglobulin A and (5) carbonic anhydrase I. Capillary, 30 cm total length, 50 pm 0.d. and 192 pm 0.d.; applied voltages, 20 kV for
injection and separation, 5 kV for electrospray; buffer, 20 mM e-arninocaproic acid titrated with acetic acid at pH 4.4; sheath liquid,
methanol-water-acetic acid (50 : 49 : 1, v/v/v) at pH 2.6, 5 pI rnin-' ; mass scan, m/z 700 to 2000 at 2.5 s per scan.
Figure 4. Positive electrospray ionization mass spectrum of myoglobin taken from the average scans under the peak in Fig. 3.
Q. TANG, A. KAMEL HARRATA AND C. S. LEE
particularly useful approach involving on-column iso-
tachophoretic sample preconcentration has recently
reported by Tinke et a1.14 and Karger and co-
workers. 15,16 With the proper selection of running
buffers and the on-column combination of capillary iso-
tachophoresis and CZE, the concentration detection
limits for a full-scan CZE/ESMS analysis was reduced
Capillary isoelectric focusing/electrospray mass
Considering the effect of arnpholyte concentration on
the CIEF separation and the protein electrospray ion-
ization mass spectra,* a solution containing 0.5% Phar-
malyte 3-10 and model proteins was used in the
CIEF/ESMS measurements. The reconstructed ion elec-
tropherograms of model proteins which were eluted by
cathodic mobilization and by combining cathodic
mobilization with a gravity-induced hydrodynamic flow
are shown in (Figs 5 and 6), respectively. In comparison
with CIEF-UV measurement (see Fig. 2), the presence
of a moving ionic boundary during cathodic mobi-
lization led to inversion of the migration order between
p-lactoglobulin A (PI 5.1) and carbonic anhydrase I (PI
6.6) (see Fig. 5). The presence of moving ionic boundary
also resulted in loss of separation resolution of myoglo-
bin variants (PI 7.2 and 6.8) in CIEF/ESMS.
In this study, the formation of a moving acetate ion
boundary from the sheath liquid was minimized by
introducing a gravity-induced hydrodynamic flow
together with cathodic mobilization of the focused
protein zones in the CIEF capillary. By comparing the
Figure 5. ClEFiESMS reconstructed ion electropherogram of 5 x
M each of (1) cytochrome c (pl 9.6), (2) ribonuclease A (pl 9.4),
(4) myoglobin (pl 6.8), (5) B-lactoglobulin A (pl 5.1) and (6)
carbonic anhydrase I (pl 6.6). Capillary, 20 cm total

CIEF/ESMS measurements shown in Figs 5 and 6, sig-
nificant enhancement in the separation resolution, par-

On the other hand, there was no significant difference
in the separation resolution of CIEF/UV measurements
under the influence of additional gravity mobilization.
The cathodic mobilization of focused protein zones was
initiated by replacing the sodium hydroxide catholyte
with a sheath liquid in the CIEF/UV experiments (see
Fig. 2). Thus, the existence of acetate ion boundary
might be at or very close to the cathodic end of the
CIEF capillary and possibly explained the lack of the
moving ionic boundary effect in the CIEF/UV separa-
The acidic and basic carrier ampholytes focused near
both ends of the CIEF capillary might be insufficient to

provide smooth and continuous pH gradients for CIEF
protein separation. The lack of smooth pH gradients
contributed to the increase in band broadening of basic
proteins including cytochrome c (PI 9.6) and ribonu-
clease A (PI 9.4) in CIEF/ESMS (see Fig. 6). Further-
more, the differences in the migration time between the
protein peaks during cathodic mobilization did not
truly correspond to their differences in pI value. The
non-linear velocity gradient during cathodic mobi-
lization resulted in a greater separation among basic
proteins than that of acidic proteins with similar PI dif-
ference. By comparing the results shown in Figs 3 and 6,
the concentration detection limit for a full-scan CIEF/
ESMS was reduced by a factor of 20-50. The focusing
effect of CIEF permitted the analysis of dilute protein
samples with a typical concentration factor of 50-100-
fold. However, the suppression of electrospray protein
